subtopic: Green Climate Fund and India's NDCs
title: Global Climate Funds and Initiatives
focus: Key international climate funds and partnerships including GCF, ARISE, Blue NDC Challenge, and LeadIT.
rationale: These distinct international climate mechanisms and partnerships are grouped together to provide a solid base for matching or statement-based questions on global climate architecture.
sectionExcerpt: Covers the Green Climate Fund (established COP 16, 50% mitigation/adaptation split), ARISE investment program (implemented through CIF), Blue NDC Challenge (launched by Brazil and France to integrate oceans into NDCs), and LeadIT (launched by India and Sweden for industry transition to net-zero by 2050).
evidenceBullets: Green Climate Fund (GCF) was established at COP 16 in Cancun (2010) and mandates a 50:50 investment split between mitigation and adaptation.
ARISE is implemented through Climate Investment Funds (CIF), which comprises the Clean Technology Fund and Strategic Climate Fund.
Blue NDC Challenge was launched by Brazil and France to place oceans at the heart of NDCs, recognizing oceans as carbon sinks that absorb up to 4 times more CO2 than terrestrial forests.
Leadership Group for Industry Transition (LeadIT) was launched jointly by India and Sweden in 2019 to achieve net-zero carbon emissions from high-emitting industries by 2050.

subtopic: Green Climate Fund and India's NDCs
title: India's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) and Panchamrit
focus: India's updated NDC targets for 2030, progress achieved, and the Panchamrit strategy announced at COP26.
rationale: India's NDCs and Panchamrit are core UPSC topics. The quantitative targets, baseline years, and progress metrics are highly testable and form a coherent study block.
sectionExcerpt: Details India's updated 2022 NDCs, including reducing emissions intensity of GDP by 45% from 2005 levels, achieving 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuels, and creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3 billion tonnes. Also covers the Panchamrit commitments made at COP26 Glasgow, including the net-zero target by 2070.
evidenceBullets: India's updated 2022 NDC targets a 45% reduction in emissions intensity of GDP from 2005 levels by 2030, with 36% already achieved between 2005 and 2020.
India targets 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel-based energy by 2030, and has achieved 51% as of September 2025.
India's updated NDC includes the 'LiFE' (Lifestyle for Environment) mass movement component as a key to achieving its quantitative targets.
Panchamrit commitments at COP26 Glasgow include reaching 500 GW non-fossil energy capacity by 2030 and achieving net-zero emissions by 2070.

subtopic: Green Climate Fund and India's NDCs
title: Greenhouse Gases Emission Intensity (GEI) Target Rules, 2025
focus: The newly notified GEI Target Rules, obligated sectors, compliance mechanisms, and penalties.
rationale: A specific, newly notified domestic regulatory framework. The sectors covered, the definition of GEI, and the roles of CPCB and BEE are prime targets for objective questions.
sectionExcerpt: Outlines the GEI Target Rules 2025 notified by the Union Environment Ministry, proposing legally binding GHG emission targets for over 400 industrial units. It covers four primary energy-intensive sectors (aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, pulp & paper) and five additional sectors. Compliance is linked to the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2023, with CPCB imposing environmental compensation for shortfalls.
evidenceBullets: GEI Target Rules 2025 establish legally binding GHG emission targets for four primary sectors: aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, and pulp & paper.
GEI targets are calculated using the Bureau of Energy Efficiency's (BEE) methodology specific to each obligated entity.
Obligated entities must meet targets annually under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2023, or purchase certificates from the Indian Carbon Market (ICM).
Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) imposes environmental compensation equal to twice the average traded price of carbon credit certificates for non-compliance.
